original_hypothesis.title: Incompatible IERC20 Interface Causes Permanent DoS for Non-Standard Stablecoins
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: The contract uses the standard IERC20 interface which expects a boolean return value. Non-standard stablecoins like USDT do not return a boolean on transfer/transferFrom. The EVM will revert these calls, rendering the AMM completely unusable for the most popular USD stablecoin.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: Since the protocol is explicitly designed for 'USD stablecoins', the inability to support USDT represents a critical failure of the core business logic and prevents protocol adoption.
deployment_assumptions: Pool is created using USDT or other non-standard ERC20 tokens that do not return a boolean
original_hypothesis.config_evidence: FeeAMM.sol imports standard OpenZeppelin IERC20.sol and does not use SafeERC20.
preconditions: Target token does not return a boolean on transfer/transferFrom (e.g., USDT on mainnet)

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: Standard interface expects 32 bytes of return data, EVM reverts if 0 bytes returned
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Any interaction with the pool will revert, locking any accidentally sent funds or preventing usage
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: No direct profit, but causes complete denial of service and potential loss of utility

economic_questions: Which specific target chains and stablecoins are intended for deployment?
Are there any wrapper contracts planned to handle non-standard tokens?
validation_requirements: Test contract interactions using a mainnet fork with actual USDT contract
Verify EVM behavior on the specific target chain

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Prevents market making for major stablecoins

validation_result.impact_summary: All transfer/transferFrom calls through the IERC20 interface will revert when interacting with tokens like USDT that return no boolean, causing permanent DoS for pools using such tokens.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The FeeAMM contract imports OpenZeppelin's IERC20 interface and calls transfer/transferFrom directly without using SafeERC20. In Solidity 0.8.x (the contract uses >=0.8.13 <0.9.0, foundry.toml specifies 0.8.28), when an external call is made through an interface that declares a `bool` return type, the ABI decoder expects exactly 32 bytes of return data. Non-standard tokens like USDT on Ethereum mainnet have transfer/transferFrom functions that return void (0 bytes of return data). When the Solidity ABI decoder receives 0 bytes but expects a bool, it triggers a revert. This means any pool created with USDT (or similar non-standard tokens) as either userToken or validatorToken will be completely unusable — all executeFeeSwap, rebalanceSwap, mint, and burn operations will revert. The contract's NatSpec explicitly states it is designed for 'USD stablecoins', and USDT is the largest USD stablecoin by market cap. The pool creation is fully permissionless with no token validation, so anyone could create a pool with USDT and it would be permanently broken.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The contract uses 7 direct IERC20 transfer/transferFrom calls across 4 functions, none of which use SafeERC20. The import at line 3 confirms only the interface is imported. No SafeERC20 import exists anywhere in the contract. Solidity 0.8.28 ABI decoder behavior will revert on empty return data when a bool is expected.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: Both transfer and transferFrom are called through IERC20 interface expecting bool return. If userToken or validatorToken is USDT (which returns void), the ABI decoder will revert due to 0 bytes of return data.
Same issue - IERC20 interface calls will revert with non-standard tokens that don't return a boolean.
If validatorToken is USDT, no liquidity can ever be provided to the pool.
If either token is non-standard, liquidity providers cannot withdraw their funds.

calling_sequence: 2. poolId computed, amountOut calculated, reserve checks pass
3. IERC20(usdtAddress).transferFrom(msg.sender, address(this), amountIn) is executed at line 106
4. Solidity 0.8.28 ABI decoder expects 32 bytes of return data (bool)
7. Entire transaction reverts, function is permanently unusable for this pool
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The vulnerability exists at every IERC20 call site. The Solidity compiler (0.8.28 per foundry.toml) generates ABI decoding logic that checks returndata size. USDT transfer/transferFrom functions are declared without return values in the original USDT contract (function transfer(address _to, uint _value) public). When called through IERC20 which declares returns (bool), the generated code does: if returndatasize() < 0x20 { revert }. Since USDT returns 0 bytes, this check fails and the transaction reverts.

deployment_assumptions_checked: Pool is created using USDT or other non-standard ERC20 tokens that do not return a boolean
config_validation.deployment_evidence: No deployment scripts specifying token addresses are provided. foundry.toml only specifies compiler settings (solc 0.8.28). The contract NatSpec mentions 'TIP-20 USD stablecoins' on 'Tempo' which is unverifiable. However, pool creation is fully permissionless — any address can call mint() with any token pair, so the vulnerability is exploitable regardless of intended deployment tokens.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: No attacker action is needed beyond attempting normal protocol usage. Any user or integrator who creates a pool with USDT (mainnet) or similar non-boolean-returning tokens will find the pool permanently broken. All four state-mutating functions (executeFeeSwap, rebalanceSwap, mint, burn) will revert. If tokens are accidentally sent to the contract address directly, they are permanently locked since no rescue mechanism exists.
validation_result.impact_assessment: HIGH severity. For any pool using a non-standard ERC20 token: (1) mint() reverts — no liquidity can be provided, (2) executeFeeSwap() reverts — no fee swaps possible, (3) rebalanceSwap() reverts — no rebalancing possible, (4) burn() reverts — if any tokens were somehow deposited (e.g., via direct transfer), they are permanently locked. The contract explicitly targets USD stablecoins, and USDT is the #1 USD stablecoin. This is a fundamental compatibility defect that prevents the protocol from serving its stated purpose with the most important token in its target market.
recommended_mitigations: Import and use OpenZeppelin's SafeERC20 library: import '@openzeppelin/contracts/token/ERC20/utils/SafeERC20.sol'; using SafeERC20 for IERC20;
Replace all IERC20(token).transfer(...) calls with IERC20(token).safeTransfer(...)
Replace all IERC20(token).transferFrom(...) calls with IERC20(token).safeTransferFrom(...)
SafeERC20 handles both cases: tokens returning bool and tokens returning void (0 bytes)
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Fork Ethereum mainnet. 2. Deploy FeeAMM. 3. Attempt to call mint() with USDT (0xdAC17F958D2ee523a2206206994597C13D831ec7) as validatorToken. 4. Observe revert. 5. Repeat with USDT as userToken in executeFeeSwap. 6. Confirm all interactions revert. 7. Deploy same test with SafeERC20 to confirm fix works.
uncertainties: The NatSpec references 'TIP-20 USD stablecoins' on 'Tempo' — if this is a custom chain where all stablecoins follow standard ERC20 (returning bool), the practical impact on the intended deployment may be reduced, though the code defect still exists
The exact deployment chain is not specified in available configuration
additional_testing_needed: Test on mainnet fork with USDT, BNB, and other known non-standard ERC20 tokens
Verify behavior on target 'Tempo' chain if TIP-20 token specifications are available
Test with tokens that return false instead of reverting (separate but related SWC-104 issue)
